comments.content:
Greetings JMG,

In the GD system, given that the work is structured into successive grades of initiation, is it be possible for someone practicing consistently for an extended period of time to 'outpace' a certain grade, even though they may never have been formally initiated into it (either through self-initiation or more traditional forms)? For example, can someone doing a standard course of practice such as the LBRP + MP + divination + discursive meditation eventually perform at a rate above Neophyte, even though they may never have been initiated into it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Good heavens, yes.

The thing to keep in mind is that the grade structure of any order is to some extent arbitrary -- it's one way of trying to see to it that each student in that order gets a good general education in occult philosophy and practice. If a student takes up the basic practices and performs them relentlessly, especially if their practices include discursive meditation and they also put some time into reading and study of occult books, they're going to end up learning a great deal about occultism and achieving spiritual development, whether or not they do it according to some specific system of grades.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If I performs the basic practices relentlessly (including discursive meditation) and put significant time into reading and study of occult books, what am I missing out on if I never self-initiate through the various grades (or go through a temple initiation)?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The advantages of initiation rituals, self- or otherwise, are that they give you a boost in your development and enable you to make use of certain energies linked to the symbols of the degree. It's as if you were making a long journey on foot, and every so often you got to a place where a bus covers part of the route; you can catch the bus, rest your feet for a while, and get off a few miles further along, or you can just keep walking. You'll get to your destination either way, but taking the initiatory bus when you can gets you there a little sooner and makes the trip a little easier.

comments.content:
I did a geomantic reading on what the results would be from a hiking event I'm planning. The chart came out exceedingly complicated and I won't ask for an interpretation of it - but it's a good opportunity to ask some questions on which parts of the chart are relevant and which aren't if I want to calculate the indicators for the chart.

[IMAGE:0] (if the image doesn't load, it looks like you can click the link and access the image)

1. Tristitia is in conjunction and company compound with the quesited in the fifth house. I note that Tristitia is also in the seventh house. Does Tristitia in the sixth house, since it is also a significator in this chart, form a conjunction with Tristitia in the seventh house? If that's so, I assume that means I must also include the effects of Tristitia in the seventh house in my calculations
of the indicators and in my interpretation of the reading.

2. Tristitia in the twelth house counts as a translation since it's conjunct with Laetitia in the first and fifth houses, correct?

3. You said in The Art and Practice of Geomancy that aspects only come into play when one of the significators passes to a different house. Laetitia is in the first, fifth, and ninth houses. Do I count only the trine aspect between the first and ninth houses, or do I count *both* the first-fifth trine and the first-ninth trine in my calculations? Same question would apply to the square and opposing aspects between Laetitia and Tristitia here.

4. As Tristitia is a significator in this chart, does that mean Populus - which is in company of houses with Tristitia in the eight house, and is also in company and conjunction with Laetitia in the first house - also count as a significator?

Sorry if this is a lot of questions, just trying to understand what parts of the chart do figure into the calculations/readings and which don't.

Much thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The most common source of inaccurate interpretations of geomantic readings is overthinking them. You asked a fifth house question; your significator in the first passes to the fifth, and it's a very favorable figure -- Laetitia. This chart perfects by occupation, the strongest mode of perfection. The answer to your question is "yes" -- and you can stop right there.

Now of course this chart *also* perfects by translation, with Tristitia in the 6th and 12th, and it has Laetitia in the 9th trine both significators. Those simply reinforce the basic meaning, which is "yes." The crucial point, though, is that the answer to your question is determined by the presence or absence of at least one mode of perfection between querent and quesited. The rest is much less important, and when there's a definite answer given by the mode of perfection, the rest doesn't really matter.

replies.content:
Hi Open Space,

Your comments about navigation reminded me of something I read in an interview with Ian Mackaye (Fugazi, Minor Threat) about a month or so ago. They resonated with me and I made a note of it... here is what he said, with a link to the full interview. He was asked a question about "survival", which was the theme of the issue for the magazine, and this is what he had to say in response...

"It’s the word survival – the idea you would ‘survive’ something. I understand that people, melodramatically, may consider life something one has to survive. But you’re alive, that’s what life is, you are surviving. It plays into this idea that people’s lives are narratives – that it’s a film or book and you have to survive all this craziness. I think it’s a disservice, ultimately, because it makes others feel like their lives aren’t crazy enough.

In my mind, life is not a war – although human beings create conditions that make it feel that way – and I think that navigation is a fairer term. I see life essentially as an empty field. The construct of that empty space has to do with society, but it also has to do with us. The only real question is how are we going to navigate that space, from beginning to end. If people thought of themselves as navigators, maybe they would have more purchase. Navigation is about having a say in the matter, whereas surviving is about dealing with things being thrown at you. With navigation you get to decide whether you want to be in that situation in the first place."

https://www.huckmag.com/art-and-culture/music-2/ian-mackaye-survival-issue-interview/

Happy meditations, and it's good to look at this theme again myself.

JPM

replies.title: Navigating / Return to the Trunk
replies.content:
Open_Space,

I'm glad you found that useful. I thought some more of the article as I went about my day and recalled he also mentioned something that he found useful from C.S. Lewis that he had read in his twenties...

"There was this image of life as a tree and each decision we made was a branch. And then every decision we made, once we were on that branch, were smaller branches and smaller branches until you got down to the twigs. The author explained that if you are on the wrong branch, if you made a bad decision, you have to go back to the trunk – because once you’re on that branch, every decision will be wrong. That was such a great thing for me. I was just navigating, I made a mistake, so I have to go back to the trunk. Because back at the trunk, life – simple life – is always right."

So yeah, navigating, and returning to the trunk as needed, before going on a different branch.

I like the images you posted to anon as well up above and I also like what you said here too:

"removing the hooks that make us think we have to be running like rats all the time to pursue artificial goals to arrive at somewhere we can consider 'safe' is perhaps one of the greatest forms of rebellion."

It can be weird when you don't chase the same rats as everyone else who is running around. But it can also be peaceful.

This is also useful to me as a reminder that when the prevailing winds aren't blowing towards the direction I've set for myself, their is probably a trick or too that sailors know for staying on course.

comments.title: Pathworking
comments.content:
Hello, JMG.

Questions:

1. When doing pathworkings on the tree of life, why is it important to return to the sphere of origin?

2. Can I "chain" several paths for a longer journey? Is there a benefit to doing that?

3. There should be a path between Chesed and Binah, but I couldn't find any notes about it. A hint, please?

4. Are there any dangers to pathworking on the tree of life?

replies.title: Re: Pathworking
replies.content:
1) To ground yourself and avoid becoming too spaced out.

2) I don't recommend it. That usually results in not paying enough attention to each path.

3) No, there is no such path. You can't get from Chesed directly to Binah. You have to return to Tiphareth and start from there.

4) If you take the imagery too literally, and forget that it's all symbolic, you can go off the deep end mentally to some extent. That's the only danger I know of.

replies.content: What happens if you try to take a path on the Tree of Life that doesn't exist?

replies.title: Re: Pathworking

replies.title: Re: Pathworking

I am truly delighted to say that I keep seeing evidence that one of my predictions for 2023 is coming true. The prediction is that we will see a huge wave of interest in serious Western occultism and perhaps discursive meditation cresting in the wake of a dying interest in materialist monotheism.

There are a few things that have been notable here. Number one was Greg Reese of Infowars effectively rehashing/shortening a Mark Passio lecture on de facto Satanism. Now, anyone who is knitting their underpants in frenzied anticipation of a new Satanic Panic should take a chill pill. Passio's schtick is to point out how materialist Christianity and materialism in general is essentially worshipping the other side. Passio is an ex-Satanist who inveighs against Satanism and encourages serious study of the occult.

The second synchronicity is a bit more personal and has to do with the Orphic Hymns. There was a weird night about a month ago where I went to a local open mic here in the western suburbs of Chicago, borrowed a guitar, and sang some Orphic Hymns because that is what I have ready and practiced. They went over surprisingly well, and since it was a slow night, I did have a moment to explain what they were. There has been another surprising positive development that I will reveal only if it all works out with the Hymns -- suffice to say there is far more interest in a bunch of folk songs about the Greek gods with super-nerdy lyrics than I expected.

The third and fourth synchronicity can be bunched together -- there are a couple of Bitchuters reposting occultists and esoteric materials. One was a link to Youtube occultist Theoria Apophasis and the other was this video that mentions the Essenes and Manly P. Hall:

Magic is in the air... specifically Western magic. Fingers crossed that this trend continues!

replies.title: Re: Connecting with Ancestors and Spirits of house and land
replies.content:
You might check out the book Ancestral Medicine by Daniel Foor. There are exercises in the book that help you to discern ancestral connections and then establish relationship(s) with your ancestors (along with the proper boundaries.) You stated that you already call on your ancestors for aid - I'd suggest appending that and calling on only your well-healed ancestors, or some other signifier that would exclude unbalanced ancestors. In some indigenous traditions ancestors are associated with the West and with water, and this also aligns with the fall being the time of Samhain and Dia de Los Muertos.

For land spirits, heathen traditions have specific practices and rituals tied to land taking (claiming not only the land you will inhabit, but in doing so also acknowledging where your domain ends,) and to acknowledging and honoring spirits of the land (like erecting cairns.) I find that there is much to be gained from "spending quiet time with" - meaning, before you go following other's practices for your land, maybe spend some time just being there and observing it, you may learn more specifically how to honor your land spirits.

replies.title: Re: Connecting with Ancestors and Spirits of house and land
replies.content:
This is something that I am still working out for my own practice, but some works that have been helpful for me (note that these are mostly pretty Germanic-focused, which may or may not suit your needs):

1) The Soul's Inner Statues is a pleasantly reasonable guide to polytheistic practice written by a Neoplatonist Hellenic polytheist. The whole book is available free online, but here's a link to an especially relevant section: https://kayeofswords.github.io/soulsinnerstatues/foundations.html#hearth-home-and-ancestors

2) Galina Krasskova has written about ancestor worship from a mostly-Germanic polytheist standpoint, but her practice is influenced by African diaspora religions. I believe I got the basics of her take on ancestor worship either from Northern Tradition for the Solitary Practitioner or A Modern Guide to Heathenry, but her standalone book on the matter is Honoring the Ancestors: a Basic Guide.

3) True Hearth by James Chisholm is written for followers of Asatru, but it has some good thoughts on where to give worship to your household spirits (if, for example, you don't have an actual hearth), some rituals for "landtaking", which among other things is meant to get you right with the land spirits (and settle with them that you belong here), and some tips on putting together a household shrine.

4) It's very common in Germanic prayer/ritual to include a toast to ancestors as part of a drink offering to deities. For example, you might be giving a drink to Odin, but as part of the offering you say something like "and in the name of my forebearers who followed you on the path of wisdom, I toast." It is also fairly common to make the pouring out of drink offerings after a ritual into a mini-offering to the land spirits. My approach is to ask the deity for leave to remove the drink offering from the ritual space once it's over, then when I pour it out I over it to the land spirits with the deity's leave and my blessings, if they want them.

5) I don't have a specific work to point you to, but ancestor worship is still a normal part of everyday life for many Japanese folks, some of whom consider themselves "Shintoists", others "Buddhists", and some with no particular religious label, so you might find some sources there helpful. I found the posts on Green Shinto about ancestor worship at least interesting, though I don't know how much they have shaped my practice: https://www.greenshinto.com/category/ancestor-worship/

If you want to dig further into shintoism, I put together a bunch of links shared by the community here: https://sanepolytheism.dreamwidth.org/3448.html

Good luck!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Connecting with Ancestors and Spirits of house and land
replies.content: Oh, I also forgot to mention Demons and Spirits of the Land and The Tradition of Household Spirits by Claude Lecouteux, which I haven't read yet, but which are more about the history of such beliefs and practices rather than a "how to", but drawing on a lot of European, primarily Germanic, folklore.

replies.title: Re: Connecting with Ancestors and Spirits of house and land
replies.content:
Agree with jprussel that looking into Japanese culture can give you a starting point on building a home altar. For Japanese, anything concerning the afterlife is associated with Buddhism while Shinto is more concerned with dealing with the here and now so info below is Buddhist in nature.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Butsudan

- A 仏壇 / Butsudan is the altar.
- Wife's family follows Nichiren so in their 仏壇 they have a symbol holy to the Nichiren displayed within.
- There is also a "prayer book" of some holy Nichiren sutras.
- There is also a family / ancestor record book (goes back many hundreds of years).
- Incense holder - light incense first
- A small "standing bell" to let your ancestors know you are about to prayer or communicate https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standing_bell. One tap is good.
- No cremation ashes are kept in here. May be different for others.

I'd say you could repurpose the above to fit your needs / beliefs. Holy symbol of your choice, holy book. If, like me, you don't have ancestral records going back hundreds of years I'd say use whatever object you have on hand you feel puts you closer to your ancestors.

comments.title: “Queer Gnosticism”
comments.content: Greetings, JMG! Author James Lindsey has written extensively about Critical Theory and other social phenomena. Lately, he has been talking about CT in terms like “Queer Gnosticism”, describing “spells” practitioners use to further their cause. Being an avowed atheist, he is new to this realm. Perhaps he does actually intuit the grubby magic practiced by followers of “woke” in particular and the Religion of Progress generally, but lacks the knowledge of how to describe it properly. All this is a data point, but it made me think, what god or pantheon is the Religion of Progress interacting with? My own sense is that there is some death-obsessed cult driving this but I couldn’t guess at what deities are involved. It also has an odd parasitic quality that allows it to latch on to other organizational systems as anything can be “progressed”. I’m stumped as to what’s behind it.

replies.title: Re: “Queer Gnosticism”
replies.content:
I'm sorry to see Lindsay picking up Eric Voegelin's use of gnosticism as a polemic term; he's got a distorted understanding of the nature of gnosis, and it weakens what might otherwise be a solid critique. As for the religion of progress, that leads into interesting territory. One of the things that happens routinely in the late stages of a civilization is that worship increasingly gets directed away from actual divine powers and toward egregors -- that is to say, collective representations created and charged by a culture, with no connection to the transcendent.

The classical world was full of those -- temples to the emperor, temples to abstract qualities such as Peace and Fortune, and so on. Progress is one of ours, though there are others; Dialectical Materialism had quite a few worshippers back in the day, nations have their temples, and half of what went wrong with pop-culture Wicca is that while the old traditional Wiccans worshipped deities, the pop Wicca launched on its way by Starhawk and Margot Adler worshipped an egregor, "the Goddess," who was basically nothing more than the projected self-image of Second Wave feminism. One problem with egregors, in turn, is that their emotional and spiritual charge depletes, they turn into clichés, and after a while it becomes painfully clear to everyone that there's nobody home. The other problem is that their followers so often make promises the egregors can't keep.

replies.title: Re: “Queer Gnosticism”
replies.content: I wonder if that might not also be something that happened with the so-called "Religious Right" in the United States?  The Jesus, or Jesii, they worshipped was an egregor built in their own intolerant, neocon images?

replies.title: Re: “Queer Gnosticism”
replies.content: That's quite possible, and it would explain why they also guttered out in the usual way.

replies.title: Re: “Queer Gnosticism”
replies.content: Huh, very interesting. Can egregores ever act as vehicles for actual divine powers?

replies.title: Re: “Queer Gnosticism”
replies.content: Yes, and in fact that's how religion works -- the egregor of a deity becomes a vehicle for the divine power. What sets a real religion apart from the kind of thing we're discussing is that real religions are born out of religious experience: people have personal encounters with a divine power, their experiences become the template for an egregor, and the egregor becomes a meeting ground through which the divine and the human interact. In a fake religion, the egregor is created by human beings for human purposes, and the divine never comes into the picture at all.

replies.title: Re: “Queer Gnosticism”
replies.content: How can one tell if a particular religious organization is dealing with an authentic deity or a (hu)man-made egregor?  Or is that something that only comes apparent with the passage of time (which is little aid to earlier worshippers)?

They're not even trying to dial up the right god(s).

replies.content:

comments.title: Just a little spark
comments.content:
I woke up this morning from a dream in which I understood the tarot to have a fifth suit, named “Time”. That’s proving to be an interesting jumping off point for me. I started cogitating on what the Ace of Time would represent. My first thought was the Big Bang, but that didn’t seem like a good fit with the broader tarot character. I remembered last week’s conversation about time not really existing per se, just being an effect of our limited perceptions and linear awareness. I decided that the Ace could embody the first instant of “perception”, since in order to experience time one needs to perceive something.

Anyway, it occurred to me that this could be a very fruitful way to approach deepening my understanding of the tarot. In order to create a new suit, I would need a deep understanding of the way the cards embody their qualities (what are twos about? threes?). How would that play out in my new suit? Also, it’s fun to mess around with! I thought I’d throw that out here to see if it sparked for anyone else the way it did me.

Thank you as always, JMG, for your hosting and guidance.

Squirrelly Jen

replies.title: Re: Just a little spark
replies.content:
The idea of a Time suit sparked many thoughts for me; I'm thankful for your comment.
The first place I went was in thinking of a time suit as only existing within divination, not separately. First I thought of this as a practice, meaning - I'd lay out a spread face down and then begin the reading. As I progressed, I'd read each card and note the time spent on each, and use that knowledge as another layer of interpretation of each card's meaning in relation to my question.

Second idea is to note the order of the cards within their suit and use that relation to understand their temporal nature. I.e. if I drew three cards for how a choice would play out, if I drew two fives and then the seventh card of the major arcana, then I'd interpret the fives to be concurrent consequences and the major arcana card to happen at a later time in relation.

Final thought is that the time suit is inherent in all the suits and the major arcana through their ordering. The concepts of each card can be seen as a transition from the card before it to the card that follows it.

Hoping you have as much fun with this as I'm having. :)

replies.title: Re: Just a little spark
replies.content: Hmm! That's a fun idea. The only tarot deck I know of that has five suits is the Discordian deck, which has the suits of Sweet, Boom, Prickle, Pungent, and Orange, named for the five Discordian elements.

replies.title: Re: Just a little spark
replies.content: I'll offer that Piers Anthony presented a five suit deck - the Spirit Tarot - in his Gods of Tarot Series of 1979. A portion of the work is presented as pathworking meditaions of various cards, at least one of which was considered controversial of its time, and an appendix addressed musings of working with the additional tarot suit

replies.title: Re: Duc De Palatine?
replies.content: There's a whole complicated subculture of people who have noble titles from countries that no longer exist, or were granted titles by monarchies that have been abolished. That scene has considerable overlaps with various occult and alternative spiritual scenes, and it's not uncommon for occultists and independent bishops to receive titles from the members of the aristocratic fringe. I suspect Duc de Palatine was one of the many titles invented and conferred during Napoleon's day, though it might have been during one of the other French invasions of Germany -- there were a lot of those back in the day.

comments.content:
Hi, JMG:

It seems like you can't pick up a book on the history of occultism without seeing a picture of Eliphas Levi's Baphomet, with a variety of explanations of what it is and what it represents. I'm wondering your opinion on this entity, if it even is an entity. I know it's come to represent the Devil for some. For others, it represents occult wisdom. replies.title: Re: Baphomet
replies.content: I've had some small experiences with him after a talisman with his image was hung around my neck during a meditation about Solomon's Temple with the instructions to meditate on the meaning (I was flipping out when I looked down and very clearly saw what it was). I would say he is very strong medicine only to be taken 'by prescription ' as it were. replies.title: Re: Baphomet
replies.content:
Lévi didn't intend to create an entity, or even an egregor. His drawing is a pantacle -- that is, a symbolic design meant to represent the universe:



It's still worth study and meditation. The difficulties came in because, first, it was picked up by clueless Christian zealots who wanted to shriek "The Devil! The Devil!" Then it was picked up by clueless Satanists, who as usual got all their ideas from the Christians; and then it was picked up by clueless Neopagans; and from all these sources it entered popular culture, and developed an unusually tangled egregor. All this gets in the way of its meaning.

Baphomet is the material universe in its two expressions, physical and etheric. It is birth and sex and death, it is life in incarnation, it is Life. Its secret is concealed in its name; if you write Baphomet in Hebrew letters and put it through the Cabalistic process known as Temurah, in which one letter is replaced by another letter according to some fixed scheme -- the scheme in question is called Atbash, and it's the most widely used form of Temurah -- it comes out as Sophia, Wisdom. So the material universe is the veil that conceals divine wisdom. That's the secret of the Templars, fwiw...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
What is the difference between "gnostic" as used to describe the independent sacramental churches you read about in occult contexts (such as the one John Gilbert brought you into) and first century Gnosticism? I'm guessing that not everyone who self-describes as gnostic or belongs to a tradition with "gnostic" in its name believes that a nasty rogue deity created the physical universe and that being trapped in a fleshly body is an inherently terrible thing; if I'm correct, what does "gnostic' mean when it doesn't mean that?

While I'm asking, what do the two have to do with each other? What do they have in common? Did these traditions at least have the original gnostics in mind when they chose the name, even if the actual beliefs differ?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First of all, the ancient Gnostics weren't all world-hating dualists. Some were, of course, but others had different beliefs. The Naassenes, for example, held that the world was as good as a material place could be, and encouraged initiates to pass through Greek agricultural mysteries (such as the Eleusinian mysteries) as part of their training.

What unites all Gnostics is, as the name suggests, a focus on gnosis. This is knowledge, but not book-learning. Bentley Layton, one of the best modern scholars of Gnosticism, translates it as "recognition." A metaphor may help. Imagine that you had a passionate year-long romance with someone who was in the witness protection program. Every "fact" your lover told you was a fiction, so in one sense you know nothing about her -- but in another sense, you know her very, very well. That latter sense is gnosis. That's the kind of knowledge gnostics seek to have of the spiritual world: not dogma, not book-learning, but personal experience. A spiritual path that seeks that experience is a gnostic path.
